concepts.title: Conditional Probability
concepts.conceptBody:
Conditional probability

If A & B are 2 events related to an experiment then the probability of occurrence of A under the condition that B has already occurred etc. i.e. relative to B, is known as conditional probability. 

It is denoted by $\text{P}\left( \text{A}/\text{B} \right)$ or $\text{P}(\text{A}$ given $\text{B})$.

Note here $\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)\ne 0$ is must.

What do we mean by this?

Let us take an experiment to consider it. Two dices are thrown and 2 events A & B are recorded.

A: The sum of dices is 8 .

B: There is an odd number on first die.

So now if we need to find the probability of occurence of event A such that B has already occurred will be called conditional probability. "A given B".

i.e. $\text{P}\left( \text{A}/\text{B} \right)$

Now we can imagine from the above written lines that the sample space for $\text{P}\left( \text{A}/\text{B} \right)$ will become the possible outcomes of event B.

And the favourable events become the intersection of $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$, as $\text{B}$ has to be taken in consideration as result of $\text{A}$ is based on $\text{B}$.

Hence the formula becomes

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}/\text{B} \right)=\frac{\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)}{\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)}$

Let us complete the example we have taken

B = $\text{ Occurence }\text{ of }\text{ odd }\text{ on }\text{ first }\text{ die }$

= $\{\left( 1,1 \right)\left( 1,2 \right)\left( 1,3 \right)\ldots \left( 1,6 \right)$

$\left( 3,1 \right)\left( 3,2 \right)\left( 3,3 \right)\ldots \left( 3,6 \right)$

$\left( 5,1 \right)\left( 5,2 \right)\left( 5,3 \right)\ldots \left( 5,6 \right)\}$

= $18\text{ cases }$

A = $\text{ occurence }\text{ of }\text{ sum }8$

= $\left\{ \left( 2,6 \right)\left( 3,5 \right)\left( 4,4 \right)\left( 5,3 \right)\left( 6,2 \right) \right\}$

Note : Here $\left( 2,6 \right)$ and $\left( 6,2 \right)$ are 2 different cases and first represents 2 on dice 1 and 6 on dice 2. Next represents 6 on dice 1 and 2 on dice 2.

$\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)=\frac{5}{36}\text{ and }\text{ P}\left( \text{B} \right)=\frac{18}{36}=$$\frac{1}{2}$

$\text{n}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)=2$ {$\text{ as }\text{ sample }\text{ space }=36,\text{ when }2\text{ dices }\text{ are }\text{ rolled }\}$

$\{\text{ possible }\text{ cases }\text{ are }\left( 3,5 \right)\And \left( 5,3 \right)$

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)=\frac{2}{36}=\frac{1}{8}$

Hence $\text{P}\left( \text{A}/\text{B} \right)=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)}{\text{n}\left( \text{B} \right)}=\frac{2}{18}=\frac{1}{9}$

$\text{ Or }=\frac{\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)}{\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)}=\frac{1}{18\times \frac{1}{2}}=\frac{1}{9}$

For independent events : If $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ are two independent events then

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}/\text{B} \right)=\frac{\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)}{\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)}=\frac{\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)}{\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)}=\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)$

As $\mathbf{P}\left( \mathbf{A}\cap \mathbf{B} \right)=\mathbf{P}\left( \mathbf{A} \right).\mathbf{P}\left( \mathbf{B} \right)$

Similarly, for $\text{P}\left( \text{B}/\text{A} \right)=\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)$

concepts.title: Multiplication Theorem
concepts.conceptBody:
Multiplication Theorem 

If $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ are two events associated with a random experiment, then

$\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)=\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right).\text{P}\left( \text{B}/\text{A} \right)$

Or $\text{P}\left( \text{B}\cap \text{A} \right)=\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right).\text{P}\left( \text{A}/\text{B} \right)$

This can be just derived from conditional probability.

Note: This theorem is used in cases where the events occur in different stages and not simultaneously.

EXTENSION OF THE THEOREM 

$\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}}\cap {{\text{A}}_{2}}\cap {{\text{A}}_{3}} \right)=\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}} \right).\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{2}}/{{\text{A}}_{1}} \right).\text{P}\left( {{\text{A}}_{3}}/\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}}\cap {{\text{A}}_{2}} \right) \right.$ and this can be generalized for $\text{n}$ such events

Illustration 

If A and B are two events such that $P\left( A \right)=0.5,P\left( B \right)=0.6$ and $P\left( A\cup B \right)=0.8$, find P( A / B ) and $\mathbf{P}\left( \mathbf{B}/\mathbf{A} \right)$. 

Solution: 

We have, $\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cup \text{B} \right)=\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)-\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)=\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)-\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cup \text{B} \right)=0.5+0.6-0.8=0.3$

Now, $\text{P}\left( \text{A}/\text{B} \right)=\frac{\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)}{\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)}\Rightarrow \text{P}\left( \text{A}/\text{B} \right)=\frac{0.3}{0.6}=\frac{1}{2}$

And $\text{P}\left( \text{B}/\text{A} \right)=\frac{\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)}{\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)}\Rightarrow \text{P}\left( \text{B}/\text{A} \right)=\frac{0.3}{0.5}=\frac{3}{5}$

Illustration

A die is rolled twice and the sum of the numbers appearing on them is observed to be 7. What is the conditional probability that the number 2 has appeared at least once? 

Solution: 

Consider the following events:

$\text{A}=$ getting number 2 at least once; $\text{B}=$ getting 7 as the sum of the numbers on two dice. We have

$\text{A}=\left\{ \left( 2,1 \right),\left( 2,2 \right),\left( 2,3 \right),\left( 2,4 \right),\left( 2,5 \right),\left( 2,6 \right),\left( 1,2 \right),\left( 3,2 \right),\left( 4,2 \right),\left( 5,2 \right),\left( 6,2 \right) \right\}$

and $\text{B}=\left\{ \left( 2,5 \right),\left( 5,2 \right),\left( 6,1 \right),\left( 1,6 \right),\left( 3,4 \right),\left( 4,3 \right) \right\}$

$\therefore \text{ P}\left( \text{A} \right)=\frac{11}{36},\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)=\frac{6}{36}\text{ and }\text{ P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)=\frac{2}{36}$

Now, required probability

$=\text{P}\left( \text{A}/\text{B} \right)=\frac{\text{P}\left( \text{A}\cap \text{B} \right)}{\text{P}\left( \text{B} \right)}=\frac{2/36}{6/36}=\frac{1}{3}$
